{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985
== External links == *[http://english.tsukudo.jp/feast.html Mikoshi Photos of Shinto shrine] (English version) *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070927034006/http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/mikoshi_festival_matsuyamas_fa.html Mikoshi Festival] *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=318 Shin'yo, in the Encyclopedia of Shinto by the Kokugakuin University] {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category|Mikoshi}}

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]

Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mikoshi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mikoshi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mikoshi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mikoshi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mikoshi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mikoshi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:11 2]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Mikoshi]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]
}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]
}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985
== External links == *[http://english.tsukudo.jp/feast.html Mikoshi Photos of Shinto shrine] (English version) *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070927034006/http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/mikoshi_festival_matsuyamas_fa.html Mikoshi Festival] *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=318 Shin'yo, in the Encyclopedia of Shinto by the Kokugakuin University] {{Commons Category:kk]]
[[Category|Mikoshi}}
{{shinto shrines}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]
}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985
== External links == *[http://english.tsukudo.jp/feast.html Mikoshi Photos of Shinto shrine] (English version) *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070927034006/http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/mikoshi_festival_matsuyamas_fa.html Mikoshi Festival] *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=318 Shin'yo, in the Encyclopedia of Shinto by the Kokugakuin University] {{Commons Category:kk]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]
}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985
== External links == *[http://english.tsukudo.jp/feast.html Mikoshi Photos of Shinto shrine] (English version) *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070927034006/http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/mikoshi_festival_matsuyamas_fa.html Mikoshi Festival] *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=318 Shin'yo, in the Encyclopedia of Shinto by the Kokugakuin University] {{Commons Category:kk]]

{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985
== External links == *[http://english.tsukudo.jp/feast.html Mikoshi Photos of Shinto shrine] (English version) *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070927034006/http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/mikoshi_festival_matsuyamas_fa.html Mikoshi Festival] *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=318 Shin'yo, in the Encyclopedia of Shinto by the Kokugakuin University] {{Commons Category:kk]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Palanquin Shrine in Shinto}} {{Italic title|reason=
}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden02n4592.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' of [[Hiyoshi-taisha]].]] [[File:Nada no Kenka matsuri , himeji01.JPG|thumb|''Mikoshi'' fighting on Nada-no-Kenka Matsuri at [[Himeji]].]] [[Image:ToshoguMikoshi5.jpg|thumb|This ''mikoshi'' enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] at the [[Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]]] A {{nihongo||神輿|'''''mikoshi'''''|lead=y}} is a sacred religious [[palanquin]] (also translated as portable [[Shinto shrine]]). [[Shinto]] followers believe that it serves as the vehicle to transport a deity in [[Japan]] while moving between main shrine and temporary shrine during a festival or when moving to a new shrine. Often, the ''mikoshi'' resembles a miniature building, with pillars, walls, a roof, a [[veranda]] and a railing.
Often the [[Japanese honorific]] prefix {{nihongo4||お|''o-''}} is added, making {{nihongo4||お神輿|'''''omikoshi'''''}}. == History == The first recorded use of ''mikoshi'' was during the [[Nara period]]. Among the first recorded uses was when in the year 749, the deity [[Hachiman]] is said to have been carried from [[Kyushu]] to [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] to worship the newly-constructed [[:en:Daibutsu|''Daibutsu'']] at [[Tōdai-ji]].<ref name = usa>[https://www.city.usa.oita.jp/tourist/touristspot/touristspot2/touristspot3/10171.html "Usa City Tourist Navigation"] Retrieved 6 June 2024</ref><ref>[http://www.usajinguu.com/todaiji "東大寺の大仏建立"] Usa Shrine. Retrieved 6 June 2024.</ref> As the head shrine of all [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman shrines]] in Japan, [[Usa Jingū]] in [[Ōita Prefecture]], Kyushu is said to be the birthplace of ''mikoshi''.<ref name= usa />
== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985
== External links == *[http://english.tsukudo.jp/feast.html Mikoshi Photos of Shinto shrine] (English version) *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070927034006/http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/mikoshi_festival_matsuyamas_fa.html Mikoshi Festival] *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=318 Shin'yo, in the Encyclopedia of Shinto by the Kokugakuin University] {{Commons Category:kk]]

== Shapes == [[File:JakJapanMatsuriMikoshi 2018.jpg|thumb|A ''mikoshi'' in Jak Japan Matsuri 2018]] [[File:ギャル神輿２.JPG|thumb|Woman mikoshi]] [[File:Children carry mikoshi Sanja Matsuri 2006.JPG|thumb|Children mikoshi ([[Sanja Matsuri]])]] [[File:Tomioka-hachimangu-1.jpg |thumb|Japan's largest ([[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]])]] [[File:Edo meisho dōke zukushi, Tōri-itchōme Gion-e by HiroKage.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hirokage]]]] Typical shapes are rectangles, hexagons, and octagons. The body, which stands on two or four poles (for carrying), is usually lavishly decorated, and the roof might hold a carving of a [[Phoenix (mythology)|phoenix]].
== Festival and flow == During a ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Japanese festival) involving a ''mikoshi'', people bear the ''mikoshi'' on their shoulders by means of two, four (or sometimes, rarely, six) poles. They bring the ''mikoshi'' from the shrine, carry it around the neighborhoods that worship at the shrine, and in many cases leave it in a designated area, resting on blocks called ''uma'' (horse), for a time before returning it to the shrine. Some shrines have the custom of dipping the ''mikoshi'' in the water of a nearby lake, river or ocean (this practice is called ''o-hamaori''). At some festivals, the people who bear the ''mikoshi'' wave it wildly from side to side to "amuse" the deity ([[kami]]) inside.
== Methods of shouldering == The most common method of shouldering in Japan is {{nihongo|''hira-katsugi''|平担ぎ|extra2="flat carry"}}. Bearers chant {{nihongo|''wasshoi''|わっしょい}} and may or may not toss and shake the mikoshi.
Other methods include:
[[File:Mikoshi-localshrine-2011.ogv|thumb|(video) A local shrine being carried in Japan. As it is being carried the participants chant.]]
* {{nihongo|''Edomae''|江戸前|extra2="[[:en:Edo|Edo]] style"}} is one famous way of shouldering observable at the [[Sanja Matsuri|Asakusa Sanja Festival]]. The shout is "say ya, soi ya, sah, sorya... etc". The mikoshi is swayed rapidly, up and down and a little to the right and left. * "Dokkoi | ドッコイ " is seen in [[Shonan]] in [[Kanagawa]] Prefecture. This shouldering style usually uses two poles. The mikoshi is moved up and down rhythmically, and more slowly than in the "Edomae style". One shout is "dokkoi dokkoi dokkoi sorya" and there is a song called a "Jink | lively song." * Another one is "Odawara style | 小田原担ぎ " observed in [[Odawara]] (next to the [[Hakone]]). This is a peculiar way of shouldering in which multiple mikoshis meet and run (Holy Dash). The shout is "oisah;korasah/koryasah." and there is a song called a "Kiyari", a chant traditionally sung by workmen while pulling a heavy load and also by firemen.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://n-tobi.or.jp/english/tobi-firefighter-traditions/kiyari/ |title=Kiyari }}</ref> The bearers do not sway the mikoshi. * In this "united" style, the mikoshi uses the full width of the road, moving from side to side and turning corners at full speed.
==See also== *[[Glossary of Shinto]] *[[Honden]] *[[Matsuri Float]]
== References == {{Reflist}} * Sokyo Ono, William P. Woodward, Shinto - The Kami Way, Charles E. Tuttle Company, Tokyo 1992, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}} * [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_m.html#mikoshi Basic Terms of Shinto], Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985
== External links == *[http://english.tsukudo.jp/feast.html Mikoshi Photos of Shinto shrine] (English version) *[https://web.archive.org/web/20070927034006/http://sightseeing.in-japan.jp/2007/05/mikoshi_festival_matsuyamas_fa.html Mikoshi Festival] *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=318 Shin'yo, in the Encyclopedia of Shinto by the Kokugakuin University] {{Commons Category:kk]]
